"user_view_type": "public"}}], "cve_id": "CVE-2026-40110", "cvss": {"score": null, "vector_string": null}, "cvss_severities": {"cvss_v3": {"score": 0.0, "vector_string": null}, "cvss_v4": {"score": 7.6, "vector_string": "CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:P/PR:N/UI:P/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:L/SI:L/SA:L"}}, "cwes": [{"cwe_id": "CWE-777", "name": "Regular Expression without Anchors"}], "description": "Jupyter Server uses `re.match()` to validate the Origin header against the `allow_origin_pat` configuration.

Since `re.match()` only anchors at the start of the string, an attacker who controls a domain like `http://trusted.example.com.evil.com/` passes validation against a pattern intended to match only `trusted.example.com`.

### Impact

<=2.17.0

### Patches

057869a327c46730afede3eab0ca2d2e3e74acea, 49b34392feaa97735b3b777e3baf8f22f2a14ed8 

### Workarounds

Wrap your `allow_origin_pat` value with `^` and `$`

### References

https://github.com/jupyter-server/jupyter_server/pull/603
https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.fullmatch

title: Jupyter Server has a  CORS Origin Validation Bypass via `re.match()` in `allow_origin_pat` (from huntr)
